text: The laparoscopic approach to the splenic flexure, as with the laparoscopic approach to all colon surgery, was initially performed using the traditional lateral approach with which we were familiar from open surgery. But just as the medial approach to the right colon and the sigmoid colon has revolutionized laparoscopic colon surgery, similarly, the medial approach to the splenic flexure provides an elegant and efficient means of taking down the splenic flexure laparoscopically. Full mobilization of the splenic flexure of the colon requires a high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery with preservation of the arch of Riolon. Similarly, in order to achieve full mobilization, the inferior mesenteric vein must be divided at the inferior border of the pancreas.

While trocar placement is always a matter of personal preference, the medial approach to the splenic flexure requires the placement of a high right upper quadrant trocar to be used in conjunction with the usual right lower quadrant working port. It is my usual preference to begin any resection of a low rectal cancer or foreshortened sigmoid colon with an assessment of whether mobilization of the splenic flexure is going to be necessary. If possible, I prefer to perform this at the beginning of the procedure.

The patient is positioned securely on the operating table, tilted to the right side. The surgeon elevates the omentum over the transverse colon and packs the small bowel to the right side to expose the splenic flexure. The inferior mesenteric vein is identified at the inferior edge of the pancreas, lateral to the ligament of Treitz. The peritoneum is divided below the vein, creating a dissection window underneath it. Blunt dissection is used to carefully separate tissue planes, minimizing bleeding.

The surgeon continues dissection, identifying three landmarks: the inferior mesenteric artery at the inferior edge, the left renal artery pulsations, and the inferior margin of the pancreas at the cranial extent. The lateral extent is marked by the descending colon coming into view.

The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization by incising the peritoneum along the white line of Toldt, entering the pre-created medial dissection window. The dissection is carried up to the inferior pole of the spleen. The omentum is detached from the middle of the transverse colon, allowing entry into the lesser sac. The posterior wall of the stomach is identified to confirm proper positioning within the lesser sac, and the dissection extends laterally to connect with the lateral mobilization.

The surgeon applies upward traction on the omentum to prevent tugging on the spleen, minimizing capsular injuries. The transverse colon mesentery is transected, stopping short of the left branch of the middle colic vessels. The inferior mesenteric vein is isolated at the inferior border of the pancreas and divided. The mesentery between the distal transverse colon and proximal descending colon is carefully divided, preserving the arch of Riolon and ensuring perfusion to the distal colon. The splenic flexure is fully mobilized, allowing for the distal resection to proceed.
